---
title: "Ch 3 ‚Äî Perpendiculars and Obliques"
subtitle: "The shortest path from a point to a line ‚Äî and why slants grow with their feet"
lesson-id: "kg1.perpendiculars"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 3
kiselev-chapter-title: "Perpendiculars and slants"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry ¬ß¬ß56‚Äì74 (perpendicular existence and uniqueness, slants vs. projections, right-triangle congruence)"
prerequisites: [kg1.triangle-congruence]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state the **SAS** and **ASA** congruence theorems? See
   [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd).
2. Are you comfortable proving that the bisectors of an isosceles
   triangle's apex angle bisect the base?
3. Do you know what a **right angle** is and how to mark one in a
   figure?
4. Can you state the *triangle inequality* in your own words?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A line $\ell$ horizontal across the page; a point $P$ above it. Drop
many segments from $P$ to $\ell$, hitting different feet. **The
shortest one** is the **perpendicular** ‚Äî drag $P$ around to confirm.

```{ojs}
viewof Px = Inputs.range([-4, 4], {value: 0.5, step: 0.1, label: "P: x"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof Py = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {value: 2.5, step: 0.1, label: "P: y (height above line)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 280, padX = 30, padY = 30;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-6, 6]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-1, 5]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  // line ‚Ñì at y=0
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(-6)).attr("x2", xS(6))
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(0))
    .attr("stroke", "#333").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", w - padX - 10).attr("y", yS(0) + 16)
    .attr("font-size", 12).attr("text-anchor", "end").text("‚Ñì");

  // P
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(Px)).attr("cy", yS(Py))
    .attr("r", 5).attr("fill", "#a26");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(Px) + 8).attr("y", yS(Py) - 6)
    .attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", "bold").text("P");

  // perpendicular foot F at (Px, 0)
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(Px)).attr("x2", xS(Px))
    .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(Py))
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2.5);
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(Px)).attr("cy", yS(0))
    .attr("r", 3).attr("fill", "#26a");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(Px) + 5).attr("y", yS(0) + 16)
    .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#26a").text("F (foot)");

  // five obliques to feet at Px ¬± 1, Px ¬± 2, Px ¬± 3 (bounded)
  const offsets = [-3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3];
  for (const off of offsets) {
    const fx = Px + off;
    if (fx < -5.5 || fx > 5.5) continue;
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(fx)).attr("x2", xS(Px))
      .attr("y1", yS(0)).attr("y2", yS(Py))
      .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2").attr("stroke-width", 1);
    const len = Math.hypot(off, Py).toFixed(2);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(fx)).attr("y", yS(0) + 30)
      .attr("font-size", 9).attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("fill", "#888")
      .text(len);
  }
  // perpendicular length label
  svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(Px) - 6).attr("y", yS(Py / 2))
    .attr("font-size", 11).attr("fill", "#26a").attr("text-anchor", "end")
    .text(`‚ä• length = ${Py.toFixed(2)}`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

The perpendicular (solid blue) is shorter than every slant (dashed
grey). The further the foot strays from $F$, the longer the slant
gets.

## Wonder

1. From a point $P$ above a line $\ell$, how many perpendiculars to
   $\ell$ pass through $P$? **Exactly** one, more, or none?
2. If two slants from $P$ to $\ell$ have equal length, what does
   that say about their *feet* on $\ell$?
3. The perpendicular is the shortest. Is there a longest? (Trick
   question.)
4. A *right triangle* has a $90¬∞$ angle. The two legs determine the
   hypotenuse. Are *two* right triangles with equal hypotenuses and
   one matching leg necessarily congruent?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) **Exactly one** ‚Äî and the proof uses ASA on a hypothetical
"second perpendicular". (2) Their feet are **equidistant** from the
foot of the perpendicular through $P$. (3) No ‚Äî slants can grow
arbitrarily as the foot moves. (4) **Yes** ‚Äî *hypotenuse-leg* (HL)
is a fourth congruence theorem, special to right triangles.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 ‚Äî Existence and uniqueness of the perpendicular

Given a line $\ell$ and a point $P$ not on $\ell$, show that there
is **exactly one** perpendicular to $\ell$ through $P$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
*Existence:* drop a segment from $P$ that strikes $\ell$ at a right
angle. *Uniqueness:* suppose two perpendiculars $PF$ and $PG$ from
$P$ both meet $\ell$ at right angles ‚Äî argue triangle inequality
fails.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
*Existence* (Planimetry ¬ß57). Construct the perpendicular by reflecting
$P$ in $\ell$ to obtain $P'$; the segment $PP'$ crosses $\ell$ at
the foot $F$ at right angles, by symmetry of the reflection.

*Uniqueness.* Suppose two perpendiculars from $P$ meet $\ell$ at
distinct feet $F$ and $G$. Then $\triangle PFG$ has two right angles
at $F$ and $G$. Adding the third gives $\ge 180¬∞$ before $\angle P$
is even counted ‚Äî impossible. Hence $F = G$, and the perpendicular
is unique. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß59.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 ‚Äî The perpendicular is the shortest segment

Let $PF$ be the perpendicular from $P$ to $\ell$ (so $F$ is the
*foot*). Let $PG$ be any other segment from $P$ to a point $G$ on
$\ell$, $G \ne F$. Show that $|PF| < |PG|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
$\triangle PFG$ is a right triangle with right angle at $F$. The
hypotenuse $PG$ is the side opposite the right angle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle PFG$ has $\angle F = 90¬∞$. So
$\angle P + \angle G + 90¬∞ = 180¬∞$, giving $\angle G < 90¬∞$. The
side **opposite the larger angle is longer** (a corollary of the
exterior-angle theorem from Ch 2). $\angle F$ is the largest angle,
so $|PG|$ ‚Äî opposite it ‚Äî is the longest side. In particular,
$|PG| > |PF|$. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß60.)*
</details>

### Problem 3 ‚Äî Equal slants ‚áí equal projections

From $P$ off line $\ell$, drop the perpendicular $PF$. Let $PG$ and
$PH$ be two slants of equal length to feet $G, H \in \ell$ with $G$
and $H$ on opposite sides of $F$. Show that $|FG| = |FH|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
$\triangle PFG$ and $\triangle PFH$ are right triangles sharing leg
$PF$ and having equal hypotenuses $|PG| = |PH|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both right triangles have the right angle at $F$, share leg $PF$,
and have equal hypotenuses $|PG| = |PH|$. By **hypotenuse-leg**
congruence (proved in Problem 5 below), $\triangle PFG \cong
\triangle PFH$. Hence $|FG| = |FH|$ ‚Äî equal slants project to
equally-distant feet. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß63.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 ‚Äî Longer slant ‚áî longer projection

From $P$ off line $\ell$, $F$ the foot of the perpendicular. Let
$G, H \in \ell$ with $|FG| < |FH|$, both on the same side of $F$ on
$\ell$. Show that $|PG| < |PH|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Use the Pythagorean theorem (or its inequality version): $|PG|^2 =
|PF|^2 + |FG|^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Right triangles $\triangle PFG$ and $\triangle PFH$ share leg
$|PF|$. Their other legs are $|FG|$ and $|FH|$, with $|FG| < |FH|$.
Hypotenuse squared = sum of squared legs:
$|PG|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FG|^2 < |PF|^2 + |FH|^2 = |PH|^2$.
Hence $|PG| < |PH|$. *(The Pythagorean theorem is itself proved
later in Ch 6 via similarity; for now Kiselev cites the
exterior-angle inequality from Ch 2.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 ‚Äî Hypotenuse-leg (HL) congruence

Two right triangles have equal hypotenuses and one pair of equal
legs. Show they are congruent.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Lay one on top of the other, matching the equal legs and the right
angle. Where can the third vertex go?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle A'B'C'$ have right angles at $C$
and $C'$, with $|BC| = |B'C'|$ (equal legs) and $|AB| = |A'B'|$
(equal hypotenuses). Lay $C \to C'$, $B \to B'$ aligned along the
shared leg. The right angles at $C$ and $C'$ force ray $CA$ to
align with ray $C'A'$. Now $A$ is on ray $CA$ at distance $|CA|$
from $C$; $A'$ is on the same ray at distance $|C'A'|$. By
**Pythagoras**, $|CA|^2 = |AB|^2 - |BC|^2 = |A'B'|^2 - |B'C'|^2 =
|C'A'|^2$, so $|CA| = |C'A'|$ ‚Äî and $A = A'$. Triangles congruent.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß73.)*
</details>

### Problem 6 ‚Äî Locus of equidistant points

Show that the set of points equidistant from two given points
$A, B$ is the **perpendicular bisector** of $AB$ ‚Äî that is, the line
perpendicular to $AB$ through its midpoint $M$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
*Two directions:* (i) every point on the perpendicular bisector is
equidistant; (ii) every equidistant point lies on it. Use SAS for
(i) and isosceles for (ii).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** Let $P$ be on the perpendicular bisector of $AB$, $M$ the
midpoint. $\triangle PMA$ and $\triangle PMB$: $|MA| = |MB|$
(midpoint), $\angle PMA = \angle PMB = 90¬∞$ (perpendicular), $PM$
shared. By SAS, congruent. So $|PA| = |PB|$ ‚Äî $P$ is equidistant.

**(ii)** Conversely, suppose $|PA| = |PB|$. Then $\triangle PAB$
is isosceles. The median from $P$ ‚Äî the segment to the midpoint
$M$ ‚Äî is also the **altitude** (from Ch 2 Problem P3, or the
isosceles converse). So $PM \perp AB$, meaning $P$ lies on the
perpendicular bisector. **QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß70 ‚Äî first locus
theorem.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions.**

- **Perpendicular** from $P$ to line $\ell$: the unique segment from
  $P$ to a point $F$ on $\ell$ that meets $\ell$ at a right angle.
  $F$ is the **foot** of the perpendicular.
- **Slant** (oblique) from $P$ to $\ell$: any other segment from $P$
  to a point $G \in \ell$, $G \ne F$.
- **Projection** of slant $PG$ onto $\ell$: the segment $FG$.
- **Perpendicular bisector** of segment $AB$: the line through the
  midpoint of $AB$ perpendicular to $AB$.

**Theorems (Planimetry ¬ß¬ß57‚Äì74).**

1. *Existence and uniqueness:* through a point $P$, there is exactly
   one perpendicular to a given line.
2. *Shortest segment:* the perpendicular is shorter than every slant.
3. *Equal slants ‚áî equal projections.*
4. *Longer slant ‚áî longer projection.*
5. *HL congruence:* two right triangles with equal hypotenuse and one
   equal leg are congruent.
6. *Locus theorem:* the set of points equidistant from $A$ and $B$
   is the perpendicular bisector of $AB$.

:::

A verse:

> *From a point off a line, one perpendicular alone ‚Äî the shortest path.*
> *Slants grow with their feet ‚Äî equal feet give equal slants, and so on*
> *backward.*
> *Right triangles match by hypotenuse and a leg ‚Äî a fourth way to align.*
> *And every point that's fair to two ends sits on their perpendicular spine.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> $P$ is a point and $\ell$ a line; the perpendicular from $P$ has
> foot $F$ with $|PF| = 5$. A slant $PG$ has $|FG| = 12$. Find $|PG|$.

Right triangle $\triangle PFG$, right-angled at $F$. By Pythagoras:
$|PG|^2 = |PF|^2 + |FG|^2 = 25 + 144 = 169$. So $|PG| = 13$.

**$|PG| = 13$.**

### Practice ‚Äî Warm

**W1.** From $P$, $|PF| = 4$ (perpendicular). A slant has foot
$|FG| = 3$. Find the slant length.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{16 + 9} = 5$.
</details>

**W2.** Two slants from $P$ to $\ell$ have equal length and feet on
opposite sides of the perpendicular foot. Their two feet are $7$ cm
apart. How far is each foot from the perpendicular foot?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$3.5$ cm each.** (Equal projections, opposite sides.)
</details>

**W3.** Two right triangles have hypotenuse $13$ and one leg $5$.
Are they congruent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes ‚Äî by **hypotenuse-leg**.
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Working

**P1.** Three slants from $P$ to $\ell$ have feet at $1, 4, 9$
units from the foot $F$ of the perpendicular ($|PF| = 6$). List the
slant lengths in increasing order.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{36 + 1} = \sqrt{37}$, $\sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52}$,
$\sqrt{36 + 81} = \sqrt{117}$. **Increasing as feet move away** ‚Äî
which is the *longer-slant-with-longer-projection* theorem.
</details>

**P2.** Show that the foot of the perpendicular from $P$ to $\ell$
is the **point on $\ell$ closest to $P$**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Direct application of the *shortest segment* theorem (Problem 2).
</details>

**P3.** $A$ and $B$ are two points; $\ell$ is a line not passing
through either. Find the point $X \in \ell$ minimising
$|AX| + |XB|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Reflect $A$ across $\ell$ to get $A'$. The path $A \to X \to B$ has
the same length as $A' \to X \to B$ (since $|AX| = |A'X|$ by
reflection). The shortest such path is the straight one.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Reflect $A$ in $\ell$ to $A'$. Then $|AX| = |A'X|$, so $|AX| + |XB|
= |A'X| + |XB| \ge |A'B|$ with equality iff $X$ is on segment $A'B$.
**$X$ = intersection of segment $A'B$ with $\ell$.** This is the
*Heron's reflection* problem (and the principle behind the
**law of reflection** in optics).
</details>

### Practice ‚Äî Stretch

**S1.** A right triangle has hypotenuse $c$ and legs $a, b$. Show
that the perpendicular from the right-angle vertex to the
hypotenuse has length $\dfrac{ab}{c}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Compute the area two ways: $\tfrac{1}{2} ab$ using the legs as base
and height; $\tfrac{1}{2} c \cdot h$ using the hypotenuse and the
altitude $h$ to it.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Area $= \tfrac{1}{2} ab = \tfrac{1}{2} c h$, so $h = ab/c$.
*(A staple right-triangle fact, used heavily in similarity in Ch 7.)*
</details>

**S2.** Show that the three perpendicular bisectors of a triangle's
sides **meet at one point** ‚Äî the **circumcentre**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
Take any two of the bisectors; they meet at some $O$. Show $O$ lies
on the third.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\ell_{AB}$ be the perpendicular bisector of $AB$ and $\ell_{BC}$
of $BC$. They meet at some $O$ (they are not parallel because $AB$
and $BC$ are not parallel ‚Äî they share vertex $B$). On $\ell_{AB}$:
$|OA| = |OB|$ (locus theorem). On $\ell_{BC}$: $|OB| = |OC|$. So
$|OA| = |OB| = |OC|$. The first and last give $|OA| = |OC|$, so $O$
is on $\ell_{CA}$ ‚Äî the perpendicular bisector of $CA$. **All
three meet at $O$.** *(Planimetry ¬ß72; this $O$ is the centre of
the **circumcircle** through $A, B, C$.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß60‚Äì74.

**K1.** From a point $P$ off line $\ell$, two equal slants are drawn
to feet $G$ and $H$ with $|PG| = |PH|$. Show that the perpendicular
from $P$ to $\ell$ bisects segment $GH$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
$\triangle PGH$ is isosceles with $|PG| = |PH|$. The foot of the
perpendicular from $P$ to $\ell$ is...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle PGH$ is isosceles with apex $P$. The altitude from $P$
to $GH$ bisects $GH$ (from Ch 2). The altitude from $P$ to line
$\ell$ ‚Äî which contains $G$ and $H$ ‚Äî is precisely the perpendicular
from $P$ to $\ell$. So its foot is the midpoint of $GH$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß63.)*
</details>

**K2.** Two perpendiculars from a point $P$ are dropped onto two
*different* lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$. The two feet are $F_1$ on
$\ell_1$ and $F_2$ on $\ell_2$. If $|PF_1| = |PF_2|$, show that $P$
lies on a bisector of an angle formed by $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 ‚Äî a nudge</summary>
The right triangles $\triangle PF_1 X$ and $\triangle PF_2 X$ ‚Äî
where $X$ is the intersection of $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ ‚Äî share
hypotenuse $PX$ and have equal legs.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $X = \ell_1 \cap \ell_2$. The right triangles $\triangle PF_1
X$ (right angle at $F_1$) and $\triangle PF_2 X$ (right angle at
$F_2$) share hypotenuse $PX$ and have equal legs $|PF_1| = |PF_2|$.
By **HL** (Problem 5), they are congruent. Hence $\angle F_1 X P =
\angle F_2 X P$ ‚Äî so $P$ lies on the bisector of $\angle F_1 X F_2$,
which is one of the two bisectors of the lines $\ell_1, \ell_2$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry ¬ß71 ‚Äî locus theorem for angle bisectors.)*
</details>

**K3.** A point $P$ lies inside an angle. The perpendiculars from $P$
to the two sides of the angle have equal length. Show that $P$ lies
on the bisector of the angle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
This is K2 with the angle's sides as $\ell_1, \ell_2$ and the
vertex $X$ being the angle's vertex. By the same HL argument, $P$
lies on the angle bisector. *(Planimetry ¬ß71.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I ‚Äî Planimetry*, ¬ß¬ß60‚Äì74 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K2 and K3 are the *angle-bisector locus* exercises that pair with
Problem 6's perpendicular-bisector locus.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"The perpendicular and the shortest segment are different
   things."** They are the *same* thing ‚Äî that is the content of
   Problem 2. Many students hold them as separate facts and miss the
   connection.
2. **"Hypotenuse-leg means SSA."** It does **not**. HL adds the
   constraint that the angle opposite the longer of the two given
   sides (the hypotenuse) is a *right* angle. That extra constraint
   eliminates the SSA ambiguity.
3. **"Locus = a single point."** A *locus* is the **set** of points
   satisfying a condition. It can be a line (perpendicular bisector
   in Problem 6), a circle (locus of points at fixed distance from
   a centre), or more exotic curves. *Locus is plural in
   character.*
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove the *uniqueness of the perpendicular* (Problem 1)
   by the angle-sum contradiction without notes?
2. Did the *shortest-segment* proof (Problem 2) feel like a clean
   one-step deduction from "side opposite larger angle is longer"?
3. Can you set up Heron's reflection problem (P3) on a fresh sheet
   without consulting the hint?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Parallels* (Ch 4) ‚Äî the pilot ‚Äî armed with
  perpendicular discipline.
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 6 ‚Äî the locus theorem and its locked
  bidirectional structure are the chapter's payoff.
- **0‚Äì1 yes:** Problems 1, 2, 3 are a single pedagogical unit;
  re-walk all three.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd) ‚Äî
  SSS, SAS, ASA, the isosceles triangle theorem, and the exterior-
  angle inequality (used implicitly in the slant-projection proofs).

### What this unlocks

- [`kg1.parallels`](../04-parallels/index.qmd) ‚Äî the existence of
  parallels through a point uses the *erect-perpendicular*
  construction from this chapter.
- **`kg1.quadrilaterals`** *(Ch 5, planned)* ‚Äî diagonals of a
  rectangle / rhombus require perpendicular-bisector reasoning.
- **`kg1.circles`** *(Ch 6, planned)* ‚Äî the locus
  *equidistant-from-centre* defines a circle; tangent-radius
  perpendicularity is the next chapter's theme.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I ‚Äî Chapter 3: Perpendiculars and slants
  (¬ß¬ß56‚Äì74).** Givental translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`cbse9.triangles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd)
  (right-triangle congruence in CBSE idiom) and
  [`t2.pythagorean-theorem`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)
  (the right-triangle leg-relation our slants depend on).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development of
  the locus theorems (¬ß¬ß70‚Äì72).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** *Closest-point-on-a-line-segment* is the
single most-called geometric query in physics engines and pathfinding.
Its answer is exactly the perpendicular foot ‚Äî clipped to the segment
when the foot falls outside its endpoints. Without this lesson's
core fact you cannot write a working collision detector.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The *locus characterisation* of geometric objects ‚Äî
"the set of points satisfying property X is curve Y" ‚Äî is the seed
of analytic geometry, where curves are defined by *equations* (which
encode the locus property). Descartes' great move was to pair the
synthetic locus with an algebraic equation; this lesson gives the
synthetic side.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the ¬ß¬ß56‚Äì74 ordering and the angle-sum
  uniqueness argument); Hadamard (the *Touch* widget framing).
- **The HL theorem (Problem 5)** is given here using Pythagoras ‚Äî
  Kiselev defers the formal Pythagoras proof to similarity (Ch 7),
  but uses the legs-and-hypotenuse identity as a working tool from
  Ch 3 onward. We follow that order.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove the *concurrence of
  altitudes* (the orthocentre); that's a Ch 6 result.
:::
